title: Aqua Security Trivy Supply Chain Compromise CVE-2026-33634 (CISA KEV, Deadline April 9): TeamPCP Backdoors 76 GitHub Action Version Tags — 1,000+ Cloud Environments Infected, 500K+ Credentials Exfiltrated via Poisoned Security Scanner
affected_products: Trivy Docker Hub images v0.69.5, v0.69.6, and latest tag

sources.name: Aqua Security Blog — Trivy Supply Chain Attack: What You Need to Know
Docker Blog — Trivy Supply Chain Compromise: What Docker Hub Users Should Know
NHS England Digital — Cyber Alert CC-4758
Microsoft Security Blog — Detecting, Investigating, and Defending Against the Trivy Supply Chain Compromise
StepSecurity — Trivy Compromised a Second Time: Malicious v0.69.4 Release
Socket.dev — Trivy Under Attack Again: GitHub Actions Compromise
Wiz Research — Trivy Compromised by TeamPCP Supply Chain Attack
CrowdStrike — From Scanner to Stealer: Inside the Trivy-Action Supply Chain Compromise
Unit 42 — TeamPCP Multi-Stage Supply Chain Attacks
GitGuardian Blog — Secret Exposure Analysis: Trivy's March Supply Chain Attack
BleepingComputer — Trivy Vulnerability Scanner Breach Pushed Infostealer via GitHub Actions
The Hacker News — Trivy Security Scanner GitHub Actions Compromise: 75 Tags Hijacked
The Register — 1,000+ Cloud Environments Infected Following Trivy Compromise

timeline.event: TeamPCP's hackerbot-claw autonomous bot exploits a misconfigured pull_request_target GitHub Actions workflow in the Trivy repository, stealing an org-scoped Personal Access Token (ORG_REPO_TOKEN) with write access to 33+ Aqua Security repositories
Aqua Security detects and discloses initial breach; attempts credential rotation — but non-atomic rotation process allows attackers to intercept newly issued tokens during the rotation window

Attacker uses surviving aqua-bot service account token to force-push 76 of 77 trivy-action version tags and all 7 setup-trivy tags to malicious commits containing credential-stealing payloads (~17:43 UTC)

Malicious Trivy v0.69.4 binary released across all channels: GitHub Releases, Docker Hub, GHCR, Amazon ECR Public, deb/rpm packages, get.trivy.dev; Socket.dev independently detects attack and raises alarm (~18:22 UTC)
Trivy v0.69.4 malicious binary removed (~3 hour exposure); setup-trivy tags remediated (~4 hour exposure); trivy-action tags fully cleaned by March 20 (~12 hour exposure)
Second wave: TeamPCP pushes malicious Docker Hub images v0.69.5, v0.69.6, and latest tag using separately compromised Docker Hub credentials (~10 hour exposure before removal)
Aqua Security's proprietary GitHub org (44 repositories) defaced within 2 minutes via automated API calls using stolen service account token; repos renamed with tpcp-docs- prefix
Microsoft Security Blog and The Register report 1,000+ cloud environments actively infected; Mandiant CTO estimates 300+ GB of data and 500,000+ credentials potentially exfiltrated
CISA adds CVE-2026-33634 to Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog with April 9, 2026 federal remediation deadline

simple_summary.what_happened: Trivy — one of the most widely used open-source security scanners in the world, trusted by DevOps teams to scan containers and infrastructure for vulnerabilities — was itself turned into a malware delivery mechanism. A threat actor group called TeamPCP exploited a misconfigured GitHub Actions workflow in the Trivy repository to steal a privileged access token, then used it to replace 76 legitimate version tags with malicious ones containing credential-stealing code. When CI/CD pipelines that pin to Trivy version tags (rather than specific commit hashes) ran their security scans, they unknowingly executed the attacker's payload. The malicious code read secrets directly from CI/CD runner memory, swept dozens of common credential locations, encrypted everything, and exfiltrated it to an attacker-controlled server — all while still running the legitimate Trivy scan to avoid detection. Over 1,000 cloud environments were infected, with an estimated 500,000+ credentials potentially stolen before the attack was contained.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any CI/CD pipeline that used trivy-action (versions v0.0.1–v0.34.2) or setup-trivy (versions v0.2.0–v0.2.6) between March 19–20, 2026, or pulled Trivy Docker Hub images during March 19–23. This covers pipelines on GitHub-hosted runners and self-hosted runners. Organizations that pin to version tags (e.g., uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@v0.28.0) were affected — only those pinning to full commit SHA hashes or using the clean v0.35.0 tag were safe. 45 of 767 prominent public GitHub repositories (100+ stars) are confirmed to have run the compromised action.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: TeamPCP's bot exploits a GitHub Actions misconfiguration (pull_request_target trigger) in Trivy's repository to steal a privileged organization-scoped access token that has write access across 33+ repositories. Step 2: When Aqua Security attempts credential rotation, attackers intercept the newly issued tokens during the non-atomic rotation window, maintaining persistent access. Step 3: Using the compromised aqua-bot service account, attackers force-push all 76 trivy-action version tags to point to malicious commits where entrypoint.sh has been replaced with credential-stealing code, while preserving the appearance of legitimate commits. Step 4: Any CI/CD pipeline that runs with the compromised trivy-action automatically executes the malicious entrypoint.sh which reads Runner.Worker process memory to extract GitHub secrets that bypass masking, sweeps 50+ filesystem paths for credentials, and harvests AWS/Azure/GCP tokens, SSH keys, Kubernetes configs, Docker credentials, and more. Step 5: All harvested credentials are AES-256 encrypted and exfiltrated to scan.aquasecurtiy.org (a typosquatted domain designed to look like a legitimate Aqua scan endpoint) while the legitimate Trivy scan completes normally, making the attack completely invisible to developers.
what_you_should_do: Immediately audit all GitHub Actions workflow runs from March 19–23, 2026 — any pipeline that ran trivy-action or setup-trivy during this window should be treated as fully compromised

Rotate ALL secrets that were accessible to affected workflows: GitHub tokens, AWS keys, GCP service accounts, Azure credentials, container registry tokens (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR), SSH keys, database passwords, Kubernetes service account tokens, and Terraform state credentials
Pin all GitHub Actions to full commit SHA hashes instead of mutable version tags — change uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@v0.28.0 to uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@<full-sha>
Search all GitHub organizations for repositories named or starting with tpcp-docs — their existence confirms credential exfiltration occurred
Audit CI/CD runner filesystems and developer machines for persistence: check ~/.config/systemd/user/sysmon.py and run systemctl --user list-units to find attacker-installed persistence mechanisms
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: This attack is significant beyond its immediate impact: it demonstrates that security tools themselves are high-value supply chain targets. Trivy is used specifically because it is trusted — organizations let it run in privileged pipeline contexts with access to production secrets. By compromising the scanner, attackers bypassed every defense that teams had in place. The attack was sophisticated: it intercepted secrets from memory (bypassing GitHub's own masking), used a legitimate scanner's execution context to hide the payload, and employed a typosquatted C2 domain that resembles a real Aqua Security endpoint. With 500,000+ credentials potentially stolen and 1,000+ cloud environments infected, the downstream blast radius extends far beyond organizations that directly noticed.

tactics.technique_name: Supply Chain Compromise: Compromise Software Repository
tactics.description: TeamPCP exploited a misconfigured pull_request_target GitHub Actions workflow to steal org-scoped tokens, then force-pushed malicious code to 76 trivy-action and 7 setup-trivy version tags in the Aqua Security GitHub repository.

The malicious entrypoint.sh reads /proc/<pid>/mem from the GitHub Actions Runner.Worker .NET process, directly extracting secrets from heap memory — bypassing GitHub Actions' built-in secret masking that would otherwise redact these values from logs.

Malicious payload systematically sweeps 50+ filesystem paths on CI/CD runners for SSH keys, cloud provider credentials (AWS, GCP, Azure), Kubernetes configs, Docker credentials, Terraform state files, database passwords, and CI/CD tokens.

Harvested credentials encrypted with AES-256-CBC and RSA-4096 then exfiltrated via HTTPS POST to typosquatted domain scan.aquasecurtiy.org (mimics legitimate Aqua Security). Fallback: creates public tpcp-docs GitHub repository in victim org and uploads data as release assets — using GitHub itself to bypass corporate firewalls.

tactics.technique_name: Boot or Logon Autostart Execution: XDG Autostart Entry
tactics.description: Malicious Trivy v0.69.4 binary installs ~/.config/systemd/user/sysmon.py as a systemd unit polling every 50 minutes, connecting to a C2 hosted on an Internet Computer Protocol (ICP) blockchain canister — resistant to traditional takedown.

tactics.technique_name: Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location
tactics.description: Malicious binary masquerades as PostgreSQL log process (/tmp/pglog); persistence dropper named sysmon.py mimics Windows Sysmon; C2 domain scan.aquasecurtiy.org typosquats the legitimate Aqua Security domain to blend into network traffic.

detection_guidance.summary: Detection requires auditing GitHub Actions workflow run logs for the exposure window (March 19–23, 2026), checking for the tpcp-docs exfiltration repository pattern, and hunting for persistence artifacts on developer machines and self-hosted runners. StepSecurity's trivy-compromise-scanner tool automates the most critical detection checks across GitHub organizations.

log_sources.description: Audit all workflow runs from March 19–23, 2026 that used trivy-action or setup-trivy. Look for outbound connections to scan.aquasecurtiy.org or 45.148.10.212 in runner network logs
Search for any repository named starting with tpcp-docs — this is the fallback exfiltration mechanism, indicating credentials were successfully stolen
Monitor for outbound HTTPS connections to scan.aquasecurtiy.org (note the misspelling: 'securtiy') and IP 45.148.10.212 from pipeline runners
Check for persistence artifacts: ~/.config/sysmon.py or ~/.config/systemd/user/sysmon.py, and /tmp/pglog process — indicators of the secondary payload from the malicious v0.69.4 binary

what_to_look_for.description: Attacker-controlled repository created in victim's GitHub org as fallback exfiltration channel — presence confirms successful credential theft
Primary C2 exfiltration endpoint — note the typo 'securtiy' instead of 'security'; any connection from a CI runner to this domain/IP indicates active exfiltration

what_to_look_for.indicator: Malicious entrypoint.sh size (17,592 bytes vs legitimate 2,855 bytes)
what_to_look_for.description: The malicious entrypoint.sh in compromised trivy-action versions is 6x larger than the legitimate file — significant size discrepancy in cached runner images indicates compromise

what_to_look_for.indicator: Systemd sysmon unit on developer machines or self-hosted runners
what_to_look_for.description: Persistence mechanism installed by malicious Trivy v0.69.4 binary — masquerades as Windows Sysmon but is an ICP blockchain polling backdoor

immediate_actions.action: Audit all GitHub Actions runs from March 19–23, 2026
immediate_actions.description: Identify every CI/CD workflow that ran trivy-action (versions v0.0.1–v0.34.2) or setup-trivy (v0.2.0–v0.2.6) during the exposure window. For each identified run, treat all secrets accessible to that workflow as fully compromised and rotate them. Use GitHub Actions audit log API to retrieve run history at scale.
immediate_actions.command:
# List workflow runs using GitHub CLI during exposure window
gh api repos/ORG/REPO/actions/runs --jq '.workflow_runs[] | select(.created_at >= "2026-03-19" and .created_at <= "2026-03-23") | {id: .id, workflow: .name, created_at: .created_at}'

immediate_actions.action: Rotate all credentials accessible to affected pipelines
immediate_actions.description: For every workflow run during the exposure window, rotate: GitHub Personal Access Tokens (PAT), AWS IAM keys, GCP service account keys, Azure service principals, Docker Hub tokens, GHCR tokens, ECR access keys, SSH deploy keys, Kubernetes service account tokens, Terraform backend credentials, and any other secrets available as environment variables or mounted credentials.

immediate_actions.action: Pin all GitHub Actions to commit SHA hashes
immediate_actions.description: Change all workflow files to pin GitHub Actions to full commit SHA instead of version tags. Version tags are mutable (as this attack demonstrated) while commit SHAs are immutable. Use Dependabot or StepSecurity's workflow hardening tool to automate the conversion across all repositories.
immediate_actions.command:
# Before (vulnerable — tag is mutable):
# uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@v0.28.0

# After (safe — SHA is immutable):
# uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@57a97c7e7821a5776cebc9bb87c984fa69cba8f1  # v0.35.0

# Use safe versions:
# trivy-action: v0.35.0 (SHA: 57a97c7e7821a5776cebc9bb87c984fa69cba8f1)
# setup-trivy: v0.2.6 (SHA: 3fb12ec)

immediate_actions.action: Search GitHub org for tpcp-docs exfiltration repositories
immediate_actions.description: Check all GitHub organizations for repositories named or prefixed with 'tpcp-docs' — the attacker's fallback exfiltration mechanism. If found, this confirms credential theft occurred and indicates which organization's secrets were exposed.
immediate_actions.command:
# Search for attacker exfiltration repositories
gh api search/repositories --method GET -f q='org:YOUR_ORG tpcp-docs' --jq '.items[].full_name'

immediate_actions.action: Scan developer machines and self-hosted runners for persistence
immediate_actions.description: The malicious Trivy v0.69.4 binary (if pulled to developer machines or self-hosted runners) installs a systemd persistence backdoor. Check for sysmon.py dropper and pglog masquerading process. Remove if found and rotate all credentials on the affected machine.
immediate_actions.command:
# Check for persistence dropper
ls -la ~/.config/sysmon.py
ls -la ~/.config/systemd/user/sysmon.py
# Check for running persistence
systemctl --user list-units | grep sysmon
# Check for masquerading process
ps aux | grep pglog
